key_insight: The paper improves the mixing-time guarantee for Dikin walks on polytopes from the previously best $d^{2.5}$ bound to $d^{2.25}$ by using a scaled Lee--Sidford metric and a higher-order self-concordance analysis.
engineering_relevance: This tightens the theoretical runtime of affine-invariant sampling algorithms used in convex optimization, randomized rounding, and high-dimensional probabilistic inference over polytopes.
method: Use a scaled Lee--Sidford barrier/metric to define the Dikin proposal.
Prove improved average self-concordance to raise Metropolis acceptance probability.
Control higher-order terms with selective expansions, moving orthonormal frames, and Wiener-chaos decompositions.
evidence_signal: The result is a theorem-level mixing-time proof for exponential sampling from a polytope, with warm-start complexity improved to $d^{2.25}$ and an implied better cold-start bound via annealing.
watch_item: The improvement is purely theoretical and depends on sophisticated higher-order geometric/probabilistic machinery, so practical gains may be limited by implementation complexity and barrier-geometry assumptions.
paper_title: Beyond the $d^{2.5}$-mixing bound for Dikin walks on polytopes
